reason_for_request: 根据 unresolved cluster 请求 LLM 给出注释链接修补建议

request_prompt.system: 你是 FNM 注释修补助手（同时处理 endnote 和 footnote 两种 note_system）。只处理已经确认的 unresolved cluster，不要改 section、note zone、标题或原文。优先依据页面截图判断，不要被 OCR 坏掉的数字误导。如果截图已经足够清楚，就不要退回 needs_review。你只能输出 JSON 数组；每项 action 只能是 match、ignore_ref、synthesize_anchor、synthesize_note_item 或 needs_review。match 需要 note_item_id、anchor_id、confidence、reason；ignore_ref 需要 anchor_id、confidence、reason；synthesize_anchor 仅在正文里能找到一个独一无二的短语锚点、但该锚点没有对应的结构化 anchor 记录时使用，需要 note_item_id、anchor_phrase（从正文中原样抄写的 3~12 词唯一短语，不要自编）、confidence、reason；synthesize_note_item 仅在截图里能清楚看到同页注释文本、但 OCR / 结构化流程完全没产出 note item 时使用，需要 anchor_id、marker、note_text、confidence、reason；若某条 note 当前只是错误地绑到了 synthetic / 跨页锚点，而截图清楚显示它应改绑到当前显式锚点，也直接用 match，不要 needs_review。needs_review 需要 reason。
request_prompt.user:
/no_think
下面是一个 FNM unresolved cluster。请只返回 JSON 数组，不要解释。
本次只允许动作：synthesize_anchor、needs_review。
若截图已经能明确判断坏掉的数字或同页注释，请直接输出可自动落地的动作，不要退回 needs_review。
若正文里确实能找到某条孤儿尾注对应的独一无二短语，且该短语没有对应的结构化 anchor 记录，才用 synthesize_anchor；anchor_phrase 必须逐字摘自 chapter_body_excerpt，长度 3~12 词，禁止自编、禁止填章节其他地方或通用短语。

      "ocr_excerpt": "designated madness, but I reject the possibility that feng bing referred to another kind of madness than dian 癲 and kuang 狂. ## The Qing judicial system and the documents it produced Criminal investigations usually began when someone brought an illegal act to the attention of the local magistrate, who, in addition to being the highest-ranked local administrator, was also in charge of judicial matters. When a suspicious death occurred, the magistrate brought a coroner with him to examine the corpse and ascertain the cause of death. If necessary, the magistrate also sent deputies to apprehend the killer. Although the magistrate could start to interrogate witnesses at once, the formal trial only started when the “criminal”—as a person under trial was called—and the witnesses had all been assembled. In Chinese imperial law, no indictment told a person what charges he or she faced. Instead, the magistrate investigated the “circumstances” (qing 情) of a criminal act and then decided under what law that crime should be sentenced.   "chapter_body_excerpt": "# 10 ## Prologue to Part Two Madness and mad people in the Qing dynasty have been studied mostly in a legal context. $ ^{1} $ The easy availability and relative intelligibility of legal documents vis-à-vis medical ones have undoubtedly contributed to this trend. $ ^{2} $ Following pioneers like Chen Hsiu-fen and Angelika Messner, I showed in Part One that Qing medical approaches to madness were also a legitimate topic. In Part Two, I want to show that the study of Qing legislative and judicial activities surrounding madness can be improved, and gains from being compared with the many other ways in which mad acts, words, and people were handled in both technical and non-technical contexts. In this prologue and the next few chapters, I will often propose brief comparisons with the medical field, leaving more systematic reflections for Chapter 16, the general conclusion. But before I turn to comparisons, let me first explain how the Qing legal field functioned and what kinds of documents it produced. Doing so will help to understand how the object \"madness illness\" (feng bing 瘋病) emerged from concrete legal practices. I then present the ways in which dynasties prior to the Qing dealt with homicides committed in a fit of insanity. $ ^{3} $ Then I probe the origin of the term \"madness illness\" by which Qing law designated madness, but I reject the possibility that feng bing referred to another kind of madness than dian 癲 and kuang 狂. ## The Qing judicial system and the documents it produced Criminal investigations usually began when someone brought an illegal act to the attention of the local magistrate, who, in addition to being the highest-ranked local administrator, was also in charge of judicial matters. When a suspicious death occurred, the magistrate brought a coroner ..."

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: In a “re-trial” (覆審), the prefect reviewed the entire case and could go as far as to re-interrogate the accused and all the witnesses. $ ^{5} $ Even when he agreed with the magistrate’s verdict, the prefect still had to write his own report and send it to the provincial judge, the highest legal official in the province. After having made sure that the sentence meted out was consistent with the evidence presented, the judge passed the case to the governor or

In Chinese imperial law, no indictment told a person what charges he or she faced. Instead, the magistrate investigated the “circumstances” (qing 情) of a criminal act and then decided under what law that crime should be sentenced. He then composed a report (xiang 詳) recounting the entire affair and specifying the sentence (ni 擬) that went with the law that covered this particular act. $ ^{4} $ He sent this report to his immediate superior the prefect.

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: designated madness, but I reject the possibility that feng bing referred to another kind of madness than dian 癲 and kuang 狂. ## The Qing judicial system and the documents it produced Criminal investigations usually began when someone brought an illegal act to the attention of the local magistrate, who, in addition to being the highest-ranked local administrator, was also in charge of judicial matters. When a suspicious death occurred, the magistrate brought a coroner with him to examine the corpse and ascertain the cause of death. If necessary, the magistrate also sent deputies to apprehend the killer. Although the magistrate could start to interrogate witnesses at once, the formal trial only started when the “criminal”—as a person under trial was called—and the witnesses had all been assembled. In Chinese imperial law, no indictment told a person what charges he or she faced. Instead, the magistrate investigated the “circumstances” (qing 情) of a criminal act and then decided under what law that crime should be sentenced. He then composed a report (xiang 詳) recounting the entire affair and specifying the sentence (ni 擬) that went with the law that covered this particular act. $ ^{4} $ He sent this report to his immediate superior the prefect. In a “re-trial” (覆審), the prefect reviewed the entire case and could go as far as to re-interrogate the accused and all the witnesses. $ ...

The key to the widening use of feng 瘋 to mean “madness” was its phonetic equivalence with feng 風 (“Wind”) and the age-old association of the sound feng with madness in expressions like fengkuang (風狂, “Wind madness”), fengdian (風癲, “Wind dian”), and xin feng 心風 (“Heart Wind”). By the twelfth century the sound feng (“Wind”) had been associated with madness for so long that it had come to mean madness even when it was not followed by either kuang or dian nor disambiguated by the addition of the “illness radical” (疒) to yield the form 瘋. $ ^{22} $ One “crazy monk brother” (風僧哥) was mentioned in a twelfth-century collection of anecdotes. $ ^{23} $ The term fa feng 發風 (“fit of Wind” = “fit of madness”) also appeared in a Yuan play titled A Pair of Battling Quacks (Shuang dou yi 雙鬥醫). $ ^{24} $ As mainstream medicine stopped attributing madness to Wind, $ ^{25} $ feng in the above compounds became an etymological reminder rather than an etiological marker. In other words, the term fengkuang no longer implied a belief that madness was caused by Wind, just as “lunatic” no longer refers to people who have lost their mind because of the moon. $ ^{26} $ In the late seventeenth century, for example, a physician from Huizhou in Anhui explained in his medical treatise that kuang people were often called fengzi 風子, a term that became widely used in Qing times, though it tended to be written 瘋子 ...

Feng bing (written 瘋病) may first have sounded as inelgant in Chinese as it does in its literal English translation "madness illness." The continuous use of fengdian 風顛 and fengkuang 風狂 (both written with the character for "Wind" 風) in legal documents well into the eighteenth century suggests that the form 瘋 still rarely referred to madness in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. $ ^{31} $ In 1736, one memorialist cited the law on "killing because of madness" as fengdian sharen 風顛殺人 (instead of fengbing sharen 瘋病殺人), and as late as 1816, a copyist of the Ministry of Punishments was reprimanded for writing yin feng shan as 因風殺人 (literally, "killing because of Wind") in the summary of a memorial. $ ^{32} $ A few scholars have suggested that the choice of fengbing over common medical terms like fengdian, kuang, or diankuang shows that Qing law targeted another kind of madness than medicine did. Angelika Messner (2000: 22), for example, has suggested that Qing laws did not target "insanity as illness," but a kind of non-medical "raging madness" (rasenden Wabnsinn). $ ^{33} $ This distinction does not hold. First, non-legal authors spoke of fengbing without hinting that it meant a kind of violent insanity. $ ^{34} $ Furthermore, the medical literature had long noted the homicidal potential of mad people. $ ^{35} $ Qing legal documents consistently described insanity as a medical ...
